question: What are Angular Signals and how do they improve over RxJS for component state?
shortAnswer: Signals are synchronous, fine-grained reactive primitives. Unlike BehaviorSubject, they don't need subscription management (no memory leaks), are synchronously readable (no async pipe), and integrate with Angular's change detection (OnPush-friendly). I use them for component/service state. RxJS remains for async streams (HTTP, WebSocket, complex event composition).
detailedAnswer:
Angular Signals (introduced in Angular 16+):

**What they are:**
- `signal(value)` — writable reactive value
- `computed(() => ...)` — derived value (lazy, cached)
- `effect(() => ...)` — side effect when signals change

**Advantages over RxJS for state:**
- Synchronous read: `mySignal()` — no subscribe, no async pipe
- No memory leaks: no subscription to forget cleaning up
- Fine-grained: Angular re-renders only components that read changed signals
- Simpler mental model for state management

**When to still use RxJS:**
- HTTP calls (Observable-based HttpClient)
- WebSocket streams
- Complex async composition (debounce, switchMap, merge)
- Event streams from DOM or external sources

**In this project (REWE Interview Prep):**
All services use signals for state (TopicService, QuizEngineService, InterviewService). RxJS only for route params (toSignal() bridge). Zero BehaviorSubjects.
keyPoints: Signals: synchronous, no subscription management, no leaks
RxJS still needed for async streams (HTTP, WebSocket, complex composition)
toSignal() bridges RxJS observables to signals
redFlags: "Signals replace RxJS entirely" (wrong — async streams still need RxJS)
Not knowing when to use which
"I don't know what signals are" (Angular 19 position!)
Using BehaviorSubject for simple component state

followUps.question: When would you still use BehaviorSubject over a signal?
followUps.hint: Interop with libraries expecting Observable, or complex async pipelines.

followUps.question: What is the difference between computed() and effect()?
followUps.hint: computed: derives a VALUE (pure, cached). effect: performs SIDE EFFECT (imperative, for DOM, logging).

realExperience.title: This project: 100% signals for state management
realExperience.description: Built REWE Interview Prep with Angular 19 signals throughout: TopicService (topics, questions, theory), QuizEngineService (session state), InterviewService (lazy loading per topic). Zero BehaviorSubjects. RxJS only for route params via toSignal().

question: How do standalone components work and why are NgModules no longer needed?
shortAnswer: Standalone components declare their own imports directly (what they use: other components, pipes, directives). No NgModule needed to declare/export them. Benefits: simpler mental model, tree-shakeable, easier lazy loading (loadComponent instead of loadChildren with module). In Angular 19, standalone is the default.
detailedAnswer:
Before standalone (NgModules era):
- Every component must be declared in exactly ONE NgModule
- NgModule imports other NgModules to get their components
- Circular dependency issues, unclear ownership
- Lazy loading requires a separate NgModule (route → loadChildren → module)

With standalone components:
- Component declares its own dependencies: `imports: [CommonModule, RouterLink, MyPipe]`
- No NgModule needed — component is self-contained
- Lazy loading is direct: `loadComponent: () => import('./my.component')`
- Tree-shaking: unused components not bundled

In practice (this project):
```typescript
@Component({
  selector: 'app-interview',
  standalone: true,
  imports: [MarkdownPipe],
  template: '...'
})
export class InterviewComponent { }
```

Routes:
```typescript
{ path: 'topic/:id', loadComponent: () => import('./theory.component').then(m => m.TheoryComponent) }
```

No app.module.ts exists. Zero NgModules in the entire project.
keyPoints: standalone: true — component declares its own imports
loadComponent for lazy loading (simpler than loadChildren + module)
redFlags: Still creating NgModules for everything in Angular 19
Cannot explain the migration path from NgModules
"I always use SharedModule" (often unnecessary with standalone)

followUps.question: How do you share components across features without NgModule?
followUps.hint: Import the component directly where needed. Barrel exports (index.ts) for organization.

followUps.question: When might you still want an NgModule?
followUps.hint: Third-party libraries that haven't migrated yet. Legacy code. Almost never for new code.

realExperience.title: This project: zero NgModules
realExperience.description: REWE Interview Prep has zero NgModules. Every component is standalone with explicit imports. Lazy loading via loadComponent in routes. app.config.ts replaces app.module.ts for providers.

question: How do you structure a medium-to-large Angular application?
shortAnswer: Feature-based structure: core/ (singletons), shared/ (reusable components), features/ (lazy-loaded by route). Each feature is self-contained — imports only from shared/ and core/, never between features. Services in core/ manage global state. Communication between features via services, not direct imports.
detailedAnswer:
Structure:
```
src/app/
├── core/        ← Singletons (services, guards, interceptors)
├── shared/      ← Reusable (components, pipes, directives)
├── features/    ← Lazy-loaded per route
│   ├── dashboard/
│   ├── theory/
│   ├── interview/
│   └── quiz/
└── models/      ← Pure interfaces (zero runtime)
```

Rules:
1. **Features never import from other features** — communication via core services
2. **Core services are singletons** — providedIn: 'root'
3. **Shared components are stateless** — inputs/outputs, no global state
4. **Models are pure TypeScript** — no Angular dependencies
5. **Each feature lazy-loaded** — only loads when route is accessed

This scales because:
- Adding a feature doesn't affect existing code
- Each feature can be developed independently
- Bundle size stays controlled (lazy loading)
- Testing is easy (features are self-contained)
keyPoints: Features never import between each other
Models are pure interfaces (zero runtime cost)
redFlags: Features importing directly from other features
Services with state in shared/ (should be in core/)

followUps.question: How do you handle state that multiple features need?
followUps.hint: Core service with signals. Features read via inject(). Single source of truth.

followUps.question: When would you use a monorepo with Nx?
followUps.hint: Multiple apps sharing code, or very large teams needing build boundaries.

realExperience.title: This project: feature-based architecture
realExperience.description: core/services/ (TopicService, QuizEngine, Interview, Progress), shared/ (MarkdownPipe, CodeBlock), features/ (dashboard, theory, interview, quiz, resume). Each feature lazy-loaded. Zero cross-feature imports.
